statement: Iceland's fishing fleet comprised approximately 1,600 registered vessels in 2024, of which around 700 were actively fishing. Direct employment in fishing was approximately 3,800 persons (full-time equivalents), with an additional 4,500 in fish processing, together representing roughly 4% of the total workforce.
source_name: Fiskistofa (Directorate of Fisheries) — Fleet and employment statistics
caveats: Employment figures represent full-time equivalents; seasonal and part-time participation is higher. The fleet has consolidated significantly — in 1990 there were over 3,000 active vessels and 6,000+ persons employed in fishing alone.

related_entries.statement: Fishing and fish processing together contributed approximately 8% of Iceland's GDP in 2024, down from roughly 12% in 2005 and over 16% in 1990. Fishing alone (excluding processing) accounted for about 4.5% of GDP. The sector's relative economic importance has declined steadily as the economy has diversified.

As of the 2024/2025 fishing year, the three largest quota holders in Iceland controlled approximately 45–50% of the total cod-equivalent quota. The ten largest holders controlled roughly 70%. Quota ownership has consolidated steadily since the ITQ system became permanent in 1990, when the top ten held approximately 35% of total quota.

citations.report_title: ESB og sjávarútvegurinn: Hver á að ráða hafinu við Ísland?

claims.claim_text: Á Íslandi eru löndun, skráning og rekjanleiki meðal þess strangasta sem þekkist í fiskveiðistjórnun.
